content.Text:
[Thu 2026-04-23 15:28 PDT] Add a "Smart Shelf Optimizer" feature to this ASP.NET MVC video rental app (Vidly). This is an autonomous shelf layout recommendation engine that analyzes rental patterns to optimize physical store shelf placement.

## What to create:

### 1. `Vidly/Services/ShelfOptimizerService.cs`
A service that:
- Analyzes co-rental frequency between movies (movies often rented together should be near each other)
- Calculates genre "heat" based on recent rental velocity
- Identifies "hidden gems" (high-rated but low-rental movies that deserve better shelf placement)
- Generates shelf zone assignments: "Prime Shelf" (eye-level, high traffic), "Discovery Zone" (curated hidden gems), "Genre Clusters" (co-rental groups), "Archive" (low demand)
- Produces a full shelf plan with zone assignments, adjacency recommendations, and rotation schedule
- Includes autonomous monitoring: detects when shelf arrangement is "stale" (rental patterns shifted significantly since last arrangement)
- Proactive recommendations: "Move X to Prime Shelf — demand up 40% this week", "Create Y+Z cluster — 65% co-rental rate"

Key classes/models to define inside the service file (or a separate Models file):
- `ShelfZone` enum: PrimeShelf, DiscoveryZone, GenreCluster, SeasonalSpotlight, Archive
- `ShelfAssignment` class: MovieId, MovieTitle, Zone, Position (int), Reason (string), Score (double)  
- `ShelfCluster` class: Name, MovieIds, CoRentalScore, SuggestedZone
- `ShelfPlan` class: Assignments (list), Clusters (list), Recommendations (list of ProactiveRecommendation), StalenessScore (0-100), GeneratedAt
- `ProactiveRecommendation` class: Type (enum: Move, Cluster, Rotate, Promote, Demote), Message, Priority (High/Medium/Low), ImpactEstimate

### 2. `Vidly/Controllers/ShelfOptimizerController.cs`
Following the same pattern as other controllers (e.g., TasteDnaController):
- Parameterless constructor using InMemoryMovieRepository, InMemoryRentalRepository, InMemoryCustomerRepository
- Constructor with DI
- `Index()` action → returns the full shelf plan view
- `PlanJson()` action → returns shelf plan as JSON
- `Zone(string zone)` action → returns filtered assignments for a specific zone
- `Clusters()` action → returns cluster analysis view
- `ClustersJson()` action → JSON endpoint for clusters
- `Monitor()` action → returns staleness analysis and rotation recommendations

### 3. `Vidly/Views/ShelfOptimizer/Index.cshtml`
An interactive HTML dashboard (Razor view) with:
- Store floor plan visualization using colored zones (CSS grid or Canvas)
- Each zone shows movies assigned to it with scores
- Co-rental cluster network (simple SVG or Canvas force-directed graph)
- Staleness gauge (circular progress indicator)
- Proactive recommendations panel with priority badges
- "Apply Rotation" button (simulated — just refreshes with new plan)
- Color scheme: warm tones for high-demand zones, cool for archive
- Responsive design

### Important patterns to follow:
- Use `Vidly.Repositories` namespace for repo interfaces (IMovieRepository, IRentalRepository, ICustomerRepository)
- Use `Vidly.Models` for Movie, Customer, Rental models
- Movies have: Id, Name, Genre (Genre object with Id/Name), NumberInStock, RentalPrice
- Rentals have: Id, Movie (Movie), Customer (Customer), DateRented (DateTime), DateReturned (DateTime?)
- Customers have: Id, Name, MembershipType, Birthdate
- Repos: InMemoryMovieRepository, InMemoryRentalRepository, InMemoryCustomerRepository are the concrete classes
- See TasteDnaController.cs and TasteDnaService.cs for the exact constructor pattern

### Build verification:
Run `dotnet build --no-restore -q` to make sure it compiles. Fix any errors before finishing.

Do NOT create any git commits - I will handle that.
I’m locating the existing MVC patterns first, specifically `TasteDnaController`, its service, and the in-memory repositories, so the new feature matches the project’s conventions before I add code.
`rg` isn’t available here, so I’m falling back to PowerShell file discovery and targeted reads of the controller, service, models, and view structure that this needs to fit into.

I’ve confirmed the app already has many analytics-style dashboards. Next I’m reading the relevant controller, service, models, and a comparable dashboard view so the new shelf optimizer can plug in cleanly without inventing a new pattern.

The repository shape is clear now. The key adaptation is that this codebase uses `RentalDate`/`ReturnDate` and `Movie.Rating`, not the exact property names in your prompt, so I’m building the optimizer against the actual models to keep it compiling cleanly.

I’m starting the actual code changes now. This will touch the service, controller, `Vidly.csproj`, and the new Razor views because this project uses explicit compile/content includes rather than SDK-style auto-discovery.
